title: Super test carve-out on the cards
full_text:
Labor will tell major superannuation funds that it is considering carving out venture capital, renewable energy and affordable housing from the annual performance test as soon as Friday, in a move designed to unlock more of their $4.5 trillion war chest for nation building or investments with riskier returns.
Treasurer Jim Chalmers is preparing to release a consultation paper before next Tuesday's budget on changing the superannuation performance test.
Two sources briefed on the discussions between the major super funds and the government said the centrepiece of the proposal would be assessing investment in certain areas separately from the returns of their more mainstream assets, such as infrastructure, equities and private markets.
The test administered by the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority ranks funds based on the yearly and longer-term returns on their investments, net of fees, and against a benchmark created by the regulator. If a product underperforms this benchmark by 0.5 per cent or more, it has failed, and the fund must write to its members acknowledging this. If it fails the next year, it is barred from accepting any new members.
Superannuation industry sources speaking on condition of anonymity said the government had been working on changes to the performance test since the productivity roundtable in August last year, where funds said the test constrained investing in start-ups and nation-building activities.
The proposal, to be announced as part of a broader public consultation on potential changes to the performance test, would separate the returns of investments in renewable energy, venture capital and affordable housing from other assets.
Those areas can underperform in the short term, meaning funds typically avoid them for fear they could hurt broader returns and result in failing the performance test.
People briefed on the matter said those assets could be placed in a pool called "emerging assets", and there were likely to be restrictions on how much money a fund could allocate to those areas.
The sources said the changes were also likely to include broadening the test's assessment of products on platforms such as those managed by AMP or MLC-owner Insignia.
Future Super, a retail fund that markets itself on ethical credentials, said in a white paper released on Thursday that the existing test was too backward-looking to promote investment in new technology or the energy transition.
It said the test was "working against the very members it was designed to protect", and performance should be measured sector by sector, rather than against an all-encompassing index favoured by the regulators.
"Long-payback assets like renewables, social housing and other nation-building infrastructure are structurally disadvantaged" under the testing regime, Future Super's white paper said.
While the venture capital industry is likely to welcome changes to the performance test, which encourage investment, entrepreneurs and investors are aghast that the government is planning to abolish the 50 per cent capital gains tax discount in favour of a model indexed to inflation.
The change was initially expected to apply only to property but is now likely to hit all asset classes, a move the Tech Council of Australia said would "disproportionately affect the economics of investing and working" for innovative startups.
"The TCA is working with the government to make the case that the reform should not inadvertently penalise the Australians who take the greatest risk to build new companies, or the investors who back them," the council said in an email to its members and partners on Thursday.
"Government has acknowledged the concerns we have raised, and is seeking to understand the tech sector's perspective and experience in detail."
The proposal to change the superannuation performance test comes as the government tries to convince the sprawling $4.5 trillion sector to invest in local affordable housing and defence infrastructure, efforts which have met a cool reception.
Assemble, a property developer focused on affordable housing and backed by super funds AustralianSuper and HESTA, recently halved the number of affordable apartments in two big projects in Melbourne, with AustralianSuper saying all investments had to stack up for their members.
The proposed performance test changes come amid a global push for pension funds to invest in their nation of origin. The UK passed controversial laws last week, forcing its $5.6 trillion pension sector to invest a minimum amount of capital in local companies and private assets.
However, industry experts have warned the Albanese government that any investments made by super funds would still need to be in the best financial interests of their members, in line with the sector's guiding legislation.
Last month, Chalmers indicated he would detail the government's views on the performance test before the budget.
"I have absolutely no interest in watering down or ditching the performance test ... I see it as a really crucial part of the superannuation system," Chalmers said.
His office did not respond to requests for comment on Thursday.
Edwina Maloney, AMP group executive in charge of platforms, said just 3 per cent of the market was covered by the current performance test criteria.
"An expanded test would raise red flags against the kind of underperformance we saw with First Guardian," she said.
summary: The Australian government is considering exempting venture capital, renewable energy, and affordable housing from the superannuation performance test to encourage investment in these sectors.
publication:
Chalmers is going for broke. Will we end up a poorer country?
The Australian09 May 20263,403 wordsMATTHEW CRANSTON

heading: Superannuation and Investments
key_points: Treasurer Jim Chalmers plans to release a consultation paper proposing changes to the superannuation performance test.
The proposed changes would allow certain investments to be assessed separately, potentially increasing funding for nation-building projects.
Concerns have been raised about the impact of abolishing the 50% capital gains tax discount on venture capital and startup investments.
relevance: These developments could significantly affect investment strategies and regulatory compliance for actuaries and financial services professionals managing superannuation funds.
